summary: User policy for Windows Spotlight content in the Settings app. Good for a cleaner Settings experience, but it removes Spotlight suggestions that can point to Microsoft features or settings guidance.

setting.name: Windows Spotlight on Settings
setting.casual_explanation: The Settings app can show Spotlight-based suggestions and highlights. This policy decides whether those Spotlight elements are allowed for the current user.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable it to keep Settings cleaner, less promotional, and less suggestion-driven.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it off reduces clutter and Microsoft suggestions in Settings, but it also removes some built-in guidance and promotion of related features.
setting.professional_notes: The Experience Policy CSP uses the allow-form name AllowWindowsSpotlightOnSettings, while CloudContent.admx maps the user Group Policy to DisableWindowsSpotlightOnSettings with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not force the Settings Spotlight policy for the current user.

allowed_values.label: Settings Spotlight allowed
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and Spotlight content can appear in Settings.

allowed_values.label: Settings Spotlight disabled
allowed_values.meaning: Spotlight content is blocked in Settings by policy.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes DisableWindowsSpotlightOnSettings = 1, which matches the documented Group Policy disable behavior.

windows_defaults.label: Windows user-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 version 1803 and newer supported systems

states.rationale: The user policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: The Experience Policy CSP documents the allow-form default as allowed, while the registry policy baseline remains not configured unless the policy is set.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: People who want Windows guidance and suggestions in Settings
avoid_for: Users who prefer a cleaner Settings app
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Settings to keep its ordinary Spotlight guidance behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want fewer suggestions and promotional surfaces in Settings.
tradeoffs: Spotlight content remains available in Settings.
No policy-based restriction is enforced for this surface.

recommended_profiles.label: Quiet UI
intended_for: Users who prefer a cleaner Settings app
avoid_for: Users who want Settings recommendations and guidance

states.rationale: Turn off Spotlight content in Settings through Group Policy.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the Settings app to show fewer Spotlight suggestions and promotions.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want Windows to recommend related features inside Settings.
tradeoffs: Settings becomes quieter and less promotional.
Some Microsoft guidance and feature discovery in Settings disappears.

evidence.title: Microsoft Policy CSP: AllowWindowsSpotlightOnSettings
evidence.summary: Official documentation for the allow-form policy controlling Windows Spotlight content in Settings.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft CloudContent.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps the user Group Policy to DisableWindowsSpotlightOnSettings with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0 under the CloudContent policy key.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes DisableWindowsSpotlightOnSettings = 1 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak provenance record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

decision.why: Local Microsoft CloudContent policy files document the exact user-policy surface, numeric mapping, and Settings-app behavior. The app writes the documented disabled value.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="DisableWindowsSpotlightOnSettings" class="User" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CloudContent" valueName="DisableWindowsSpotlightOnSettings">; CloudContent.adml: If you enable this policy, Windows Spotlight suggestions will no longer be shown in Settings app.
validation_proof.notes: Local official ADMX defines the exact user-policy key and value name; local ADML confirms that enabling the policy removes Windows Spotlight suggestions from the Settings app.
